title: Die sechs Schwäne
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 49. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The forty-ninth tale in the Grimms' collection (KHM 49), a redemption story built on silence. A king forced to wed a witch's daughter hides his seven children; the new queen sews magic shirts that turn the six boys into swans, and to free them their sister must sew six shirts of star-flowers and neither speak nor laugh for six years. Married to a second king, she is accused by his wicked mother of devouring her own newborns and condemned to the fire, finishing the shirts at the very last moment so that her brothers are restored — the youngest keeping a swan's wing where the unfinished sleeve should have been. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, gieng, fieng, empfieng, ward, thun/anthun, That, Heirath, verrathen, Urtheil and müßt.

sentences.t: Es jagte einmal ein König in einem großen Wald und jagte einem Wild so eifrig nach daß ihm niemand von seinen Leuten folgen konnte.
sentences.l: There hunted once a king in a great forest and chased a quarry so eagerly that none of his men could follow him.
sentences.i: Once a king was hunting in a great forest, and he chased a quarry so eagerly that none of his men could keep up with him.
sentences.n: The tale opens like Rapunzel with the fairy-tale “einmal” (once). “jagte … nach” is the separable verb nachjagen (to chase after), which takes the dative (einem Wild); the prefix nach jumps to the end. “Leuten” here means his men, his retinue, not people in general.

sentences.t: Als der Abend heran kam, hielt er still und blickte um sich, da sah er daß er sich verirrt hatte.
sentences.l: When the evening came near, he held still and looked about himself; there he saw that he had lost his way.
sentences.i: When evening drew near, he stopped and looked about him, and saw that he had lost his way.
sentences.n: “kam … heran” is herankommen (to draw near), separable. “sich verirren” = to lose one’s way, go astray. “um sich blicken” = to look about oneself.

sentences.t: Er suchte einen Ausgang, konnte aber keinen finden.
sentences.l: He sought a way out, but could find none.
sentences.n: “Ausgang” = a way out, exit (aus- out + Gang going).

sentences.t: Da sah er eine alte Frau mit wackelndem Kopfe, die auf ihn zu kam; das war aber eine Hexe.
sentences.l: There he saw an old woman with a wobbling head, who came toward him; but that was a witch.
sentences.i: Then he saw an old woman with a nodding head coming toward him; but she was a witch.
sentences.n: “Kopfe” carries the old dative -e (modern Kopf). “kam … zu” is zukommen auf (to come toward), separable. The narrator’s “aber” quietly warns us: she looks like a harmless old woman, but she is a witch.

sentences.t: „Liebe Frau,“ sprach er zu ihr, „könnt ihr mir nicht den Weg durch den Wald zeigen?“
sentences.l: “Dear woman,” he spoke to her, “can you not show me the way through the forest?”
sentences.i: “Good woman,” he said to her, “can you not show me the way through the forest?”
sentences.n: The king addresses the old woman with the respectful plural “ihr” (könnt ihr), the old polite “you” — the same form the kids use to their mother in other tales.

sentences.t: „O ja, Herr König,“ antwortete sie, „das kann ich wohl, aber es ist eine Bedingung dabei, wenn ihr die nicht erfüllt, so kommt ihr nimmermehr aus dem Wald und müßt darin Hungers sterben.“
sentences.l: “Oh yes, lord king,” she answered, “that I can well do, but there is a condition attached; if you do not fulfil it, then you come nevermore out of the forest and must starve to death therein.”
sentences.i: “Oh yes, my lord king,” she answered, “that I can indeed; but there is a condition to it: if you do not fulfil it, you will never come out of the forest, and must starve to death in it.”
sentences.n: “nimmermehr” = nevermore. “Hungers sterben” is an old genitive idiom, ‘to die of hunger’ (Hungers is the genitive of Hunger). The “wenn … so …” frame (if … then …) recurs constantly in Grimm.

sentences.t: „Was ist das für eine Bedingung?“ fragte der König.
sentences.l: “What kind of a condition is that?” asked the king.
sentences.i: “What sort of condition is that?” asked the king.
sentences.n: “was für ein …” = ‘what kind / sort of …’ — für here does not mean ‘for.’

sentences.t: „Ich habe eine Tochter,“ sagte die Alte, „die so schön ist wie ihr eine auf der Welt finden könnt, und wohl verdient eure Gemahlin zu werden, wollt ihr die zur Frau Königin machen, so zeige ich euch den Weg aus dem Walde.“
sentences.l: “I have a daughter,” said the old one, “who is as beautiful as any you could find in the world, and well deserves to become your consort; will you make her the lady queen, then I show you the way out of the forest.”
sentences.i: “I have a daughter,” said the old woman, “as beautiful as any you could find in the world, and well worthy to be your wife; if you will make her your queen, I will show you the way out of the forest.”
sentences.n: “die Alte” = the old woman (an adjective used as a noun). “wollt ihr die …, so …” is a conditional made by fronting the verb (‘if you will …, then …’). “Gemahlin” is an elevated word for wife/consort. “Walde” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Der König in der Angst seines Herzens willigte ein, und die Alte führte ihn zu ihrem Häuschen, wo ihre Tochter beim Feuer saß.
sentences.l: The king, in the fear of his heart, consented, and the old woman led him to her little house, where her daughter sat by the fire.
sentences.i: In his heart’s dread the king consented, and the old woman led him to her little cottage, where her daughter sat by the fire.
sentences.n: “willigte ein” is einwilligen (to consent), separable — the prefix ein goes to the end. “seines Herzens” is a genitive (of his heart); Herz has an irregular genitive Herzens.

sentences.t: Sie empfieng den König als wenn sie ihn erwartet hätte, und er sah wohl daß sie sehr schön war, aber sie gefiel ihm doch nicht, und er konnte sie ohne heimliches Grausen nicht ansehen.
sentences.l: She received the king as if she had awaited him, and he saw well that she was very beautiful, but she pleased him not for all that, and he could not look at her without a secret dread.
sentences.i: She received the king as though she had been expecting him, and he saw well enough that she was very beautiful; yet she did not please him, and he could not look at her without a secret shudder.
sentences.n: “empfieng” is the 1857 spelling of empfing (empfangen, to receive), with the old -ie- you also meet in gieng and fieng. “als wenn … hätte” = ‘as if she had …,’ subjunctive II of an unreal comparison. “gefiel ihm” — gefallen takes the dative person: she did not please him. “Grausen” = dread, horror.

sentences.t: Nachdem er das Mädchen zu sich aufs Pferd gehoben hatte, zeigte ihm die Alte den Weg, und der König gelangte wieder in sein königliches Schloß, wo die Hochzeit gefeiert wurde.
sentences.l: After he had lifted the girl up to himself onto the horse, the old woman showed him the way, and the king reached his royal castle again, where the wedding was celebrated.
sentences.i: When he had lifted the maiden up onto his horse before him, the old woman showed him the way, and the king came again to his royal castle, where the wedding was held.
sentences.n: “gehoben” is the past participle of heben (to lift). “aufs” = auf das. “gelangte” (gelangen) = reached, arrived at.

sentences.t: Der König war schon einmal verheirathet gewesen, und hatte von seiner ersten Gemahlin sieben Kinder, sechs Knaben und ein Mädchen, die er über alles auf der Welt liebte.
sentences.l: The king had already once been married, and had from his first consort seven children, six boys and one girl, whom he loved above everything in the world.
sentences.i: The king had already been married once, and had by his first wife seven children — six boys and one girl — whom he loved above everything in the world.
sentences.n: “verheirathet” keeps the 1857 -th- (modern verheiratet); the same silent -th- runs through Heirath, verrathen, Vertheidigung. “über alles … lieben” = to love above all else.

sentences.t: Weil er nun fürchtete die Stiefmutter möchte sie nicht gut behandeln und ihnen gar ein Leid anthun, so brachte er sie in ein einsames Schloß, das mitten in einem Walde stand.
sentences.l: Because he now feared the stepmother might not treat them well and might even do them a harm, he brought them into a solitary castle that stood in the midst of a forest.
sentences.i: Now because he feared the stepmother might not treat them well, and might even do them some harm, he brought them to a lonely castle that stood in the middle of a forest.
sentences.n: “möchte … behandeln … anthun” is subjunctive II of reported fear — what he feared she might do. “ein Leid anthun” = to do (someone) harm; “anthun” is the 1857 spelling of antun (separable an-), with the recurring -th-. “gar” here = ‘even, downright.’

sentences.t: Es lag so verborgen, und der Weg war so schwer zu finden, daß er ihn selbst nicht gefunden hätte, wenn ihm nicht eine weise Frau ein Knäuel Garn von wunderbarer Eigenschaft geschenkt hätte; wenn er das vor sich hinwarf, so wickelte es sich von selbst los und zeigte ihm den Weg.
sentences.l: It lay so hidden, and the way was so hard to find, that he himself would not have found it, if a wise woman had not given him a ball of yarn of wondrous property; when he threw it out before him, it unwound itself of its own accord and showed him the way.
sentences.i: It lay so hidden, and the way was so hard to find, that he himself could never have found it had not a wise woman given him a ball of yarn with a wondrous quality: when he threw it down before him, it unwound of itself and showed him the way.
sentences.n: “hätte … gefunden … hätte … geschenkt” is subjunctive II of an unreal condition (‘would not have found … had she not given’). A “weise Frau” is a wise-woman or cunning-woman, a benign counterpart to the witch. “wickelte … los” is loswickeln (to unwind), separable; “von selbst” = of its own accord.

sentences.t: Der König gieng aber so oft hinaus zu seinen lieben Kindern, daß der Königin seine Abwesenheit auffiel; sie ward neugierig und wollte wissen was er draußen ganz allein in dem Walde zu schaffen habe.
sentences.l: But the king went out so often to his dear children that his absence struck the queen; she grew curious and wanted to know what he had to do out there all alone in the forest.
sentences.i: But the king went out to his dear children so often that his absence caught the queen’s notice; she grew curious and wanted to know what business he had out there all alone in the forest.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging. “fiel … auf” is auffallen (to be noticed, to strike someone), separable, with the dative der Königin (‘struck the queen’). “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (became). “zu schaffen haben” = to have business, something to do.

sentences.t: Sie gab seinen Dienern viel Geld, und die verriethen ihr das Geheimnis und sagten ihr auch von dem Knäuel, das allein den Weg zeigen könnte.
sentences.l: She gave his servants much money, and they betrayed the secret to her and told her also of the ball of yarn, which alone could show the way.
sentences.i: She gave his servants a great deal of money, and they betrayed the secret to her, and told her too of the ball of yarn that alone could show the way.
sentences.n: “verriethen” is the 1857 spelling of verrieten (verrathen/verraten, to betray), with the old -th-. “die” at the head of the clause is a demonstrative pointing back to the servants (‘they’). “allein” here = ‘alone, only.’

sentences.t: Nun hatte sie keine Ruhe bis sie herausgebracht hatte wo der König das Knäuel aufbewahrte, und dann machte sie kleine weißseidene Hemdchen, und da sie von ihrer Mutter die Hexenkünste gelernt hatte, so nähete sie einen Zauber hinein.
sentences.l: Now she had no rest until she had found out where the king kept the ball of yarn, and then she made little white-silk shirts, and since she had learned the witch’s arts from her mother, she sewed a spell into them.
sentences.i: Now she had no peace until she had discovered where the king kept the ball of yarn; and then she made little white silk shirts, and since she had learned the arts of witchcraft from her mother, she sewed a spell into them.
sentences.n: “herausgebracht” (herausbringen, to find out) and “nähete … hinein” (hineinnähen, to sew in) are both separable. “nähete” is the 1857 form of nähte (nähen, to sew). The magic shirts are the engine of the tale — sewn to curse, and later sewn to redeem.

sentences.t: Und als der König einmal auf die Jagd geritten war, nahm sie die Hemdchen und ging in den Wald, und das Knäuel zeigte ihr den Weg.
sentences.l: And when the king had once ridden out to the hunt, she took the little shirts and went into the forest, and the ball of yarn showed her the way.
sentences.i: And once, when the king had ridden out hunting, she took the little shirts and went into the forest, and the ball of yarn showed her the way.
sentences.n: “auf die Jagd reiten” = to ride out hunting. Note the plain modern spelling “ging” here beside the 1857 “gieng” elsewhere — the edition is not perfectly uniform.

sentences.t: Die Kinder, die aus der Ferne jemand kommen sahen, meinten ihr lieber Vater käme zu ihnen und sprangen ihm voll Freude entgegen.
sentences.l: The children, who saw someone coming from afar, thought their dear father was coming to them and sprang toward him full of joy.
sentences.i: The children, seeing someone coming from afar, thought their dear father was coming to them, and ran to meet him full of joy.
sentences.n: “käme” is subjunctive II of reported thought — what they supposed (‘was coming’). “sprangen … entgegen” is entgegenspringen (to spring toward, to meet), separable, taking the dative ihm.

sentences.t: Da warf sie über ein jedes eins von den Hemdchen, und wie das ihren Leib berührt hatte, verwandelten sie sich in Schwäne und flogen über den Wald hinweg.
sentences.l: There she threw over each one of them one of the little shirts, and as it had touched their body, they transformed themselves into swans and flew away over the forest.
sentences.i: Then she threw one of the little shirts over each of them, and as soon as it had touched their bodies, they turned into swans and flew away over the forest.
sentences.n: “ein jedes” = each one. “sich verwandeln in” = to be transformed into (reflexive). “flogen … hinweg” is hinwegfliegen (to fly away); “hinweg” = away, off.

sentences.t: Die Königin gieng ganz vergnügt nach Haus und glaubte ihre Stiefkinder los zu sein, aber das Mädchen war ihr mit den Brüdern nicht entgegen gelaufen, und sie wußte nichts von ihm.
sentences.l: The queen went home quite content and believed herself to be rid of her stepchildren, but the girl had not run out to meet her with the brothers, and she knew nothing of her.
sentences.i: The queen went home well pleased, believing she was rid of her stepchildren; but the girl had not run out with her brothers to meet her, and the queen knew nothing of her.
sentences.n: “etwas los sein” = to be rid of something. “war … entgegen gelaufen” is entgegenlaufen (to run to meet), separable, with dative ihr. “ihm” refers to das Mädchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘it’ — read it as ‘her.’ “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Andern Tags kam der König und wollte seine Kinder besuchen, er fand aber niemand als das Mädchen.
sentences.l: The next day the king came and wanted to visit his children, but he found no one but the girl.
sentences.i: The next day the king came, meaning to visit his children, but he found no one but the girl.
sentences.n: “andern Tags” = the next day (an old genitive of time; andern is the older spelling of anderen). “niemand als” = no one but.

sentences.t: „Wo sind deine Brüder?“ fragte der König.
sentences.l: “Where are your brothers?” asked the king.

sentences.t: „Ach, lieber Vater,“ antwortete es, „die sind fort und haben mich allein zurückgelassen,“ und erzählte ihm daß es aus seinem Fensterlein mit angesehen habe wie seine Brüder als Schwäne über den Wald weggeflogen wären, und zeigte ihm die Federn, die sie in dem Hof hatten fallen lassen, und die es aufgelesen hatte.
sentences.l: “Oh, dear father,” it answered, “they are gone and have left me behind alone,” and told him that it had watched from its little window how its brothers had flown away over the forest as swans, and showed him the feathers that they had let fall in the courtyard, and that it had picked up.
sentences.i: “Oh, dear father,” she answered, “they are gone, and have left me here all alone,” and told him how she had watched from her little window as her brothers flew away over the forest as swans, and showed him the feathers they had dropped in the courtyard, which she had gathered up.
sentences.n: The girl is das Mädchen (neuter), so the pronouns are grammatically ‘it/its’ — read them as ‘she/her.’ “mit angesehen” = to watch, witness (mit ansehen); “habe … angesehen … wären … weggeflogen” stand in the subjunctive of reported speech. “aufgelesen” is auflesen (to pick up), separable.

sentences.t: Der König trauerte, aber er dachte nicht daß die Königin die böse That vollbracht hätte, und weil er fürchtete das Mädchen würde ihm auch geraubt, so wollte er es mit fortnehmen.
sentences.l: The king mourned, but he did not think that the queen had done the wicked deed, and because he feared the girl would be robbed from him too, he wanted to take it away with him.
sentences.i: The king grieved, but it never occurred to him that the queen had done the wicked deed; and because he feared the girl might be stolen from him too, he wanted to take her away with him.
sentences.n: “That” is the 1857 spelling of Tat (deed). “vollbracht hätte” is subjunctive II inside his (not-) thought. “würde … geraubt” = would be robbed (passive). “mit fortnehmen” = to take away with one (mitnehmen reinforced by fort).

sentences.t: Aber es hatte Angst vor der Stiefmutter, und bat den König daß es nur noch diese Nacht im Waldschloß bleiben dürfte.
sentences.l: But it had fear of the stepmother, and begged the king that it might stay just this one more night in the forest-castle.
sentences.i: But she was afraid of the stepmother, and begged the king that she might stay just this one night more in the forest castle.
sentences.n: “Angst haben vor” = to be afraid of (+ dative). “dürfte” is subjunctive II of dürfen, ‘might be allowed to.’ “Waldschloß” (forest-castle) keeps the 1857 ß.

sentences.t: Das arme Mädchen dachte „meines Bleibens ist nicht länger hier, ich will gehen und meine Brüder suchen.“
sentences.l: The poor girl thought, “of my staying there is no longer here, I will go and seek my brothers.”
sentences.i: The poor girl thought, “There is no staying here for me any longer; I will go and look for my brothers.”
sentences.n: “meines Bleibens ist nicht länger hier” is an old genitive idiom — literally ‘of my staying is no longer here,’ i.e. ‘there is no place for me to stay here any more.’ You meet the same construction again in the brothers’ warning below.

sentences.t: Und als die Nacht kam, entfloh es, und gieng gerade in den Wald hinein.
sentences.l: And when the night came, it fled, and went straight into the forest.
sentences.i: And when night came, she slipped away and went straight into the forest.
sentences.n: “entfloh” is the preterite of entfliehen (to flee, escape). “gieng … hinein” is hineingehen (to go in), separable.

sentences.t: Es gieng die ganze Nacht durch und auch den andern Tag in einem fort, bis es vor Müdigkeit nicht weiter konnte.
sentences.l: It went the whole night through and also the next day in one go, until it could go no further for weariness.
sentences.i: She walked the whole night through, and the next day as well, without stopping, until she could go no further for weariness.
sentences.n: “in einem fort” = without a break, continuously. “vor Müdigkeit” = for / out of weariness (vor of cause, as in ‘vor Freude,’ for joy). “nicht weiter konnte” drops the verb of motion — ‘could [go] no further.’

sentences.t: Da sah es eine Wildhütte, stieg hinauf, und fand eine Stube mit sechs kleinen Betten, aber es getraute nicht sich in eins zu legen, sondern kroch unter eins, legte sich auf den harten Boden und wollte die Nacht da zubringen.
sentences.l: There it saw a hunters’ hut, climbed up, and found a room with six little beds, but it did not dare to lay itself in one, but rather crept under one, laid itself on the hard floor and wanted to pass the night there.
sentences.i: Then she saw a hunters’ hut, climbed up, and found a room with six little beds; but she did not dare lie down in one, and crept under one instead, lay down on the hard floor, and meant to pass the night there.
sentences.n: A “Wildhütte” is a hut in the wild — here the robbers’ lodge. “sich getrauen” = to dare (reflexive). “nicht … sondern” = ‘not … but rather.’ “zubringen” (to pass, spend time), separable.

sentences.t: Als aber die Sonne bald untergehen wollte, hörte es ein Rauschen und sah daß sechs Schwäne zum Fenster hereingeflogen kamen.
sentences.l: But when the sun was about to go down, it heard a rushing and saw that six swans came flying in at the window.
sentences.i: But as the sun was about to set, she heard a whir of wings and saw six swans come flying in at the window.
sentences.n: “untergehen” = to go down, set (of the sun), separable. “Rauschen” is the rushing, whirring sound of wings. “kamen … hereingeflogen” = ‘came flying in’ (a past participle after kommen expressing manner of arrival; hereinfliegen is separable).

sentences.t: Sie setzten sich auf den Boden, und bliesen einander an und bliesen sich alle Federn ab, und ihre Schwanenhaut streifte sich ab wie ein Hemd.
sentences.l: They set themselves on the floor, and blew at one another and blew all the feathers off themselves, and their swan-skin stripped itself off like a shirt.
sentences.i: They settled on the floor, and blew at one another, and blew all their feathers off, and their swan-skin slipped off like a shirt.
sentences.n: Two separable verbs from blasen (to blow): “bliesen … an” (anblasen, to blow at) and “bliesen … ab” (abblasen, to blow off). “streifte sich ab” is abstreifen (to strip off), reflexive here. Note that the swan-skin comes off ‘like a shirt’ — a quiet echo of the star-flower shirts that will restore them.

sentences.t: Da sah sie das Mädchen an und erkannte ihre Brüder, freute sich und kroch unter dem Bett hervor.
sentences.l: There the girl looked at them and recognized her brothers, rejoiced and crept out from under the bed.
sentences.i: Then the girl looked at them and knew her brothers, and rejoiced, and crept out from under the bed.
sentences.n: Word order to watch: “sah sie das Mädchen an” — das Mädchen is the subject (‘the girl looked at them’), sie the object; the fronted verb inverts them. “sah … an” is ansehen (separable); “kroch … hervor” is hervorkriechen (to creep forth), separable.

sentences.t: Die Brüder waren nicht weniger erfreut als sie ihr Schwesterchen erblickten, aber ihre Freude war von kurzer Dauer.
sentences.l: The brothers were no less delighted when they caught sight of their little sister, but their joy was of short duration.
sentences.i: The brothers were no less delighted when they caught sight of their little sister; but their joy was short-lived.
sentences.n: “Schwesterchen” is the tender diminutive of Schwester (little sister), like the -chen / -lein diminutives everywhere in Grimm. “von kurzer Dauer” = short-lived, of short duration.

sentences.t: „Hier kann deines Bleibens nicht sein,“ sprachen sie zu ihm, „das ist eine Herberge für Räuber, wenn die heim kommen und finden dich, so ermorden sie dich.“
sentences.l: “Here there can be no staying of yours,” they spoke to it, “this is a lodging for robbers; when they come home and find you, then they murder you.”
sentences.i: “There is no staying here for you,” they said to her; “this is a robbers’ den — when they come home and find you, they will murder you.”
sentences.n: “deines Bleibens kann nicht sein” — the same genitive idiom as the girl’s own thought above (‘there can be no staying for you’). “die” fronting the clause is a demonstrative, ‘they’ (the robbers). “ermorden” = to murder.

sentences.t: „Könnt ihr mich denn nicht beschützen?“ fragte das Schwesterchen.
sentences.l: “Can you then not protect me?” asked the little sister.
sentences.i: “Can’t you protect me, then?” asked the little sister.
sentences.n: “denn” here is a softening particle in a question (‘then, after all’), not the conjunction ‘for.’ “beschützen” = to protect.

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antworteten sie, „denn wir können nur eine Viertelstunde lang jeden Abend unsere Schwanenhaut ablegen, und haben in dieser Zeit unsere menschliche Gestalt, aber dann werden wir wieder in Schwäne verwandelt.“
sentences.l: “No,” they answered, “for we can lay aside our swan-skin only for a quarter-hour long each evening, and have in this time our human shape, but then we are transformed again into swans.”
sentences.i: “No,” they answered, “for we can lay aside our swan-skin only for a quarter of an hour each evening, and have our human shape in that time; but then we are turned back into swans.”
sentences.n: “eine Viertelstunde lang” = for a quarter of an hour (lang measures the span). “ablegen” = to lay aside, take off, separable. “werden … verwandelt” = are transformed (passive of verwandeln).

sentences.t: Das Schwesterchen weinte und sagte „könnt ihr denn nicht erlöst werden?“
sentences.l: The little sister wept and said, “can you then not be redeemed?”
sentences.i: The little sister wept and said, “Can you not be set free, then?”
sentences.n: “erlöst werden” = to be redeemed, set free (passive of erlösen) — the key word of the tale: the sister’s whole task is to erlösen her brothers.

sentences.t: „Ach nein,“ antworteten sie, „die Bedingungen sind zu schwer.
sentences.l: “Oh no,” they answered, “the conditions are too hard.
sentences.n: The brothers now lay out the terms of redemption — a vow of silence joined to a labour of sewing, split across the next sentences.

sentences.t: Du darfst sechs Jahre lang nicht sprechen und nicht lachen, und mußt in der Zeit sechs Hemdchen für uns aus Sternenblumen zusammennähen.
sentences.l: You may for six years long not speak and not laugh, and must in that time sew together six little shirts for us out of star-flowers.
sentences.i: For six years you may neither speak nor laugh, and in that time you must sew together six little shirts for us out of star-flowers.
sentences.n: The redemption vow, and the heart of the tale: six years of total silence (no word, no laugh) while she sews six shirts. “Sternenblumen” (here; “Sternblumen” later) are star-flowers — asters, or in folk usage the starwort/aster gathered for such magic; the two spellings name the same flower. “zusammennähen” = to sew together, separable. “darfst” = you may, are allowed (dürfen).

sentences.t: Kommt ein einziges Wort aus deinem Munde, so ist alle Arbeit verloren.“
sentences.l: Comes a single word out of your mouth, then all the work is lost.”
sentences.i: If a single word comes out of your mouth, then all the work is lost.”
sentences.n: A conditional made by fronting the verb (“Kommt … , so …” = ‘if … comes, then …’). “Munde” has the old dative -e. This is the sword over the whole story: her silence must hold to the very last shirt.

sentences.t: Und als die Brüder das gesprochen hatten, war die Viertelstunde herum, und sie flogen als Schwäne wieder zum Fenster hinaus.
sentences.l: And when the brothers had spoken that, the quarter-hour was up, and they flew as swans out at the window again.
sentences.i: And when the brothers had said that, the quarter of an hour was up, and they flew out at the window again as swans.
sentences.n: “herum sein” (of time) = to be up, over. “flogen … hinaus” is hinausfliegen (to fly out), separable — mirroring the “hereingeflogen” (flew in) with which they arrived.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen aber faßte den festen Entschluß seine Brüder zu erlösen, und wenn es auch sein Leben kostete.
sentences.l: But the girl seized the firm resolve to redeem her brothers, even if it cost her life.
sentences.i: But the girl made the firm resolve to set her brothers free, even if it should cost her her life.
sentences.n: “einen Entschluß fassen” = to make / take a resolve (faßte keeps the 1857 ß). “und wenn es auch … kostete” = ‘even if it cost …,’ a concessive with subjunctive. The pronouns are neuter (das Mädchen), so ‘its life’ grammatically — read ‘her life.’

sentences.t: Es verließ die Wildhütte, gieng mitten in den Wald und setzte sich auf einen Baum und brachte da die Nacht zu.
sentences.l: It left the hunters’ hut, went into the middle of the forest and set itself on a tree and passed the night there.
sentences.i: She left the hunters’ hut, went into the middle of the forest, settled herself in a tree, and passed the night there.
sentences.n: “brachte … zu” is zubringen (to pass, spend time), separable. “sich auf einen Baum setzen” = to seat oneself up in a tree.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen gieng es aus, sammelte Sternblumen und fieng an zu nähen.
sentences.l: The next morning it went out, gathered star-flowers and began to sew.
sentences.i: The next morning she went out, gathered star-flowers, and began to sew.
sentences.n: “gieng … aus” (ausgehen) and “fieng an” (anfangen, in the 1857 spelling of fing an) are both separable. Here the promised “Sternblumen” appear in the shorter spelling — the same star-flowers the brothers named “Sternenblumen.”

sentences.t: Reden konnte es mit niemand, und zum Lachen hatte es keine Lust: es saß da und sah nur auf seine Arbeit.
sentences.l: Speak it could with no one, and for laughing it had no desire: it sat there and looked only on its work.
sentences.i: She could speak with no one, and had no wish to laugh: she sat there and looked only at her work.
sentences.n: The silence made concrete. “zum Lachen keine Lust haben” = to have no wish to laugh. “Reden” is fronted for emphasis (‘as for speaking …’), which inverts subject and verb.

sentences.t: Als es schon lange Zeit da zugebracht hatte, geschah es, daß der König des Landes in dem Wald jagte und seine Jäger zu dem Baum kamen, auf welchem das Mädchen saß.
sentences.l: When it had already spent a long time there, it came to pass that the king of the land hunted in the forest and his huntsmen came to the tree on which the girl sat.
sentences.i: When she had already spent a long time there, it came to pass that the king of that land was hunting in the forest, and his huntsmen came to the tree in which the girl sat.
sentences.n: “zugebracht” is zubringen again (to spend time), separable. “es geschah, daß …” = ‘it came to pass that …,’ a solemn fairy-tale turn. “auf welchem” = on which (a relative with welcher).

sentences.t: Sie riefen es an und sagten „wer bist du?“
sentences.l: They called out to it and said, “who are you?”
sentences.i: They called up to her and said, “Who are you?”
sentences.n: “riefen … an” is anrufen (to call out to, hail), separable.

sentences.t: Es gab aber keine Antwort.
sentences.l: But it gave no answer.
sentences.i: But she gave no answer.

sentences.t: „Komm herab zu uns,“ sagten sie, „wir wollen dir nichts zu Leid thun.“
sentences.l: “Come down to us,” they said, “we will do you nothing to harm.”
sentences.i: “Come down to us,” they said, “we mean you no harm.”
sentences.n: “zu Leid thun” = to do harm to (dative dir); “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun. This is a gentler cousin of the “ein Leid anthun” the king feared earlier.

sentences.t: Es schüttelte bloß mit dem Kopf.
sentences.l: It only shook with the head.
sentences.i: She only shook her head.
sentences.n: “mit dem Kopf schütteln” = to shake one’s head. “bloß” = merely, only.

sentences.t: Als sie es weiter mit Fragen bedrängten, so warf es ihnen seine goldene Halskette herab und dachte sie damit zufrieden zu stellen.
sentences.l: When they pressed it further with questions, it threw down its golden necklace to them and thought to make them content with it.
sentences.i: When they pressed her further with questions, she threw down her golden necklace to them, thinking to satisfy them with it.
sentences.n: “mit Fragen bedrängen” = to press with questions (Fragen is the plural of Frage). “warf … herab” is herabwerfen (to throw down), separable. “zufrieden stellen” = to satisfy, content.

sentences.t: Sie ließen aber nicht ab, da warf es ihnen seinen Gürtel herab, und als auch dies nicht half, seine Strumpfbänder, und nach und nach alles, was es anhatte und entbehren konnte, so daß es nichts mehr als sein Hemdlein behielt.
sentences.l: But they did not let off; there it threw down its belt to them, and when this too did not help, its garters, and little by little everything that it had on and could spare, so that it kept nothing more than its little shift.
sentences.i: But they would not leave off; so she threw down her belt to them, and when that did not help either, her garters, and one thing after another everything she had on and could spare, until she kept nothing but her little shift.
sentences.n: “ließen … ab” is ablassen (to let off, desist), separable. “nach und nach” = little by little. “anhatte” is anhaben (to have on, wear), separable. “entbehren” = to do without, spare. “Hemdlein” is the diminutive of Hemd — her last shift.

sentences.t: Die Jäger ließen sich aber damit nicht abweisen, stiegen auf den Baum, hoben das Mädchen herab und führten es vor den König.
sentences.l: But the huntsmen would not be turned away thereby, climbed up the tree, lifted the girl down and led it before the king.
sentences.i: But the huntsmen would not be put off by that; they climbed the tree, lifted the girl down, and led her before the king.
sentences.n: “sich abweisen lassen” = to let oneself be turned away. “stiegen” is the preterite plural of steigen (to climb). “hoben … herab” is herabheben (to lift down), separable.

sentences.t: Der König fragte „wer bist du? was machst du auf dem Baum?“
sentences.l: The king asked, “who are you? what are you doing on the tree?”
sentences.i: The king asked, “Who are you? What are you doing up in the tree?”

sentences.t: Aber es antwortete nicht.
sentences.l: But it answered not.
sentences.i: But she gave no answer.

sentences.t: Er fragte es in allen Sprachen, die er wußte, aber es blieb stumm wie ein Fisch.
sentences.l: He asked it in all the languages that he knew, but it stayed mute as a fish.
sentences.i: He questioned her in every language he knew, but she stayed as mute as a fish.
sentences.n: “stumm wie ein Fisch” = ‘mute as a fish,’ the German counterpart of English ‘silent as the grave.’ “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Weil es aber so schön war, so ward des Königs Herz gerührt, und er faßte eine große Liebe zu ihm.
sentences.l: But because it was so beautiful, the king’s heart was moved, and he seized a great love for it.
sentences.i: But because she was so beautiful, the king’s heart was moved, and he conceived a great love for her.
sentences.n: “ward … gerührt” = was moved (archaic ward + past participle). “des Königs Herz” is a fronted genitive (‘the king’s heart’). “eine Liebe zu jemandem fassen” = to conceive a love for someone.

sentences.t: Er that ihm seinen Mantel um, nahm es vor sich aufs Pferd und brachte es in sein Schloß.
sentences.l: He put his cloak about it, took it before himself onto the horse and brought it to his castle.
sentences.i: He put his cloak about her, took her up onto his horse before him, and brought her to his castle.
sentences.n: “that … um” is umtun (to put on, wrap about), separable; “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat. “ihm” is a dative (‘about her’), the German dative-of-possession where English uses a possessive.

sentences.t: Da ließ er ihm reiche Kleider anthun, und es strahlte in seiner Schönheit wie der helle Tag, aber es war kein Wort aus ihm herauszubringen.
sentences.l: There he had rich clothes put on it, and it shone in its beauty like the bright day, but there was no word to be got out of it.
sentences.i: Then he had rich garments put on her, and she shone in her beauty like the bright day; but not a word could be got out of her.
sentences.n: “Kleider anthun” = to put clothes on (anthun, 1857 for antun, separable). “es war … herauszubringen” — sein + zu + infinitive expresses possibility (‘there was to be got out’); herausbringen is separable.

sentences.t: Er setzte es bei Tisch an seine Seite, und seine bescheidenen Mienen und seine Sittsamkeit gefielen ihm so sehr, daß er sprach „diese begehre ich zu heirathen und keine andere auf der Welt,“ und nach einigen Tagen vermählte er sich mit ihr.
sentences.l: He set it at table at his side, and its modest looks and its demureness pleased him so much that he spoke, “this one I desire to marry and no other in the world,” and after some days he wedded himself with her.
sentences.i: He seated her beside him at table, and her modest looks and her demeanour pleased him so well that he said, “Her I desire to marry, and no other in the world,” and after a few days he wed her.
sentences.n: “Mienen” = the looks or expressions of the face; “Sittsamkeit” = modesty, seemliness. “gefielen ihm” — gefallen with the dative person again (‘pleased him’). “begehre … zu heirathen” — heirathen keeps the 1857 -th-. “sich vermählen mit” = to wed (elevated).

sentences.t: Der König aber hatte eine böse Mutter, die war unzufrieden mit dieser Heirath und sprach schlecht von der jungen Königin.
sentences.l: But the king had a wicked mother, who was dissatisfied with this marriage and spoke ill of the young queen.
sentences.i: But the king had a wicked mother, who was displeased with this match and spoke ill of the young queen.
sentences.n: A second wicked older woman takes over as the danger — here the king’s own mother. “Heirath” (marriage, match) keeps the 1857 -th-. “schlecht von jemandem sprechen” = to speak ill of someone.

sentences.t: „Wer weiß, wo die Dirne her ist,“ sagte sie, „die nicht reden kann: sie ist eines König nicht würdig.“
sentences.l: “Who knows where the wench is from,” she said, “who cannot speak: she is not worthy of a king.”
sentences.i: “Who knows where the wench comes from,” she said, “who cannot even speak — she is not worthy of a king.”
sentences.n: “wo … her ist” = ‘where she is from’ (her marks origin). “Dirne” here is the older neutral sense ‘girl, wench,’ used contemptuously. “eines König” stands so in the source for the expected genitive eines Königs; “einer Sache würdig sein” = to be worthy of something (+ genitive).

sentences.t: Über ein Jahr, als die Königin das erste Kind zur Welt brachte, nahm es ihr die Alte weg und bestrich ihr im Schlafe den Mund mit Blut.
sentences.l: After a year, when the queen brought the first child into the world, the old woman took it away from her and smeared her mouth with blood in her sleep.
sentences.i: A year later, when the queen brought her first child into the world, the old woman took it away from her and, while she slept, smeared her mouth with blood.
sentences.n: “über ein Jahr” = after a year (über of time elapsed). “zur Welt bringen” = to give birth to. “nahm … weg” is wegnehmen (to take away), separable, with dative ihr (‘from her’). “bestrich” is the preterite of bestreichen (to smear, coat); “im Schlafe” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Da gieng sie zum König und klagte sie an, sie wäre eine Menschenfresserin.
sentences.l: There she went to the king and accused her, saying she was a man-eater.
sentences.i: Then she went to the king and accused her, saying she was an ogress that devoured people.
sentences.n: “klagte … an” is anklagen (to accuse), separable. “wäre” is subjunctive II of the reported charge (‘that she was’). “Menschenfresserin” — literally a ‘people-eater,’ built on fressen (the verb for a beast devouring); the accusation is that the mute queen eats her own newborn children.

sentences.t: Der König wollte es nicht glauben und litt nicht daß man ihr ein Leid anthat.
sentences.l: The king would not believe it and did not allow that anyone did her a harm.
sentences.i: The king would not believe it, and would not suffer anyone to do her harm.
sentences.n: “litt” is the preterite of leiden, here in the old sense ‘to allow, suffer’ (litt nicht, daß … = would not permit that …). “ein Leid anthun” once more — to do (someone) harm.

sentences.t: Sie saß aber beständig und nähete an den Hemden, und achtete auf nichts anderes.
sentences.l: But she sat constantly and sewed at the shirts, and heeded nothing else.
sentences.i: But she sat there steadily, sewing at the shirts, and paid heed to nothing else.
sentences.n: “beständig” = constantly, without let-up. “nähete” is again the 1857 form of nähte. “an etwas nähen” = to sew at / work on something. “auf etwas achten” = to heed, attend to something.

sentences.t: Das nächstemal, als sie wieder einen schönen Knaben gebar, übte die falsche Schwiegermutter denselben Betrug aus, aber der König konnte sich nicht entschließen ihren Reden Glauben beizumessen.
sentences.l: The next time, when she again bore a fair boy, the false mother-in-law practised the same deceit, but the king could not bring himself to give credence to her words.
sentences.i: The next time, when she again bore a fine boy, the false mother-in-law worked the same deceit; but the king could not bring himself to believe what she said.
sentences.n: “übte … aus” is ausüben (to practise, carry out), separable — the second false accusation. “falsche” here = false, treacherous. “sich entschließen” = to bring oneself to, resolve. “Glauben beimessen” = to give credence (beimessen, separable; ihren Reden is dative, ‘to her words’).

sentences.t: Er sprach „sie ist zu fromm und gut als daß sie so etwas thun könnte, wäre sie nicht stumm und könnte sie sich vertheidigen, so würde ihre Unschuld an den Tag kommen.“
sentences.l: He spoke, “she is too pious and good for her to do such a thing; were she not mute and could she defend herself, then her innocence would come to light.”
sentences.i: He said, “She is too devout and good to do such a thing; were she not mute, and could she defend herself, her innocence would come to light.”
sentences.n: “zu … als daß …” = ‘too … for … to …’ (könnte is subjunctive). “wäre … und könnte …, so …” is an unreal condition made by fronting the verbs (‘were she … and could she …, then …’). “vertheidigen” keeps the 1857 -th-. “an den Tag kommen” = to come to light.

sentences.t: Als aber das drittemal die Alte das neugeborne Kind raubte und die Königin anklagte, die kein Wort zu ihrer Vertheidigung vorbrachte, so konnte der König nicht anders, er mußte sie dem Gericht übergeben, und das verurtheilte sie den Tod durchs Feuer zu erleiden.
sentences.l: But when the old woman the third time robbed the newborn child and accused the queen, who brought forward no word in her defence, then the king could not do otherwise; he had to hand her over to the court, and it condemned her to suffer death by fire.
sentences.i: But when a third time the old woman stole the newborn child and accused the queen, who brought forward not a word in her own defence, then the king could do no other: he had to hand her over to the court, and it condemned her to suffer death by fire.
sentences.n: The threefold fairy-tale structure lands its blow: the third accusation, unanswered by the silent queen, cannot be resisted. “neugeborne” = newborn (1857 -orne). “vorbrachte” is vorbringen (to bring forward, put forward), separable. “dem Gericht übergeben” = to hand over to the court. “verurtheilte” keeps the -th-; “den Tod … erleiden” = to suffer death.

sentences.t: Als der Tag heran kam, wo das Urtheil sollte vollzogen werden, da war zugleich der letzte Tag von den sechs Jahren herum, in welchen sie nicht sprechen und nicht lachen durfte, und sie hatte ihre lieben Brüder aus der Macht des Zaubers befreit.
sentences.l: When the day came near on which the sentence was to be carried out, there was at the same time the last day of the six years up, in which she might not speak and not laugh, and she had freed her dear brothers from the power of the spell.
sentences.i: When the day drew near on which the sentence was to be carried out, that was at the same time the last of the six years in which she might neither speak nor laugh come to an end; and she had freed her dear brothers from the power of the spell.
sentences.n: The timing is exact: the day of execution is the very last day of the six-year vow. “Urtheil” keeps the 1857 -th- (modern Urteil). “vollzogen werden” = to be carried out (passive). “herum sein” (of time) = to be up. “Zaubers” is the genitive of Zauber (spell).

sentences.t: Die sechs Hemden waren fertig geworden, nur daß an dem letzten der linke Ermel noch fehlte.
sentences.l: The six shirts had become finished, only that on the last one the left sleeve was still lacking.
sentences.i: The six shirts were finished, save that on the last one the left sleeve was still wanting.
sentences.n: “fertig werden” = to be(come) finished. “nur daß …” = ‘only / except that …’ “Ermel” is the older spelling of Ärmel (sleeve). This missing left sleeve is the tale’s planted detail — it will show on the youngest brother’s body.

sentences.t: Als sie nun zum Scheiterhaufen geführt wurde, legte sie die Hemden auf ihren Arm, und als sie oben stand und das Feuer eben sollte angezündet werden, so schaute sie sich um, da kamen sechs Schwäne durch die Luft daher gezogen.
sentences.l: When she was now led to the pyre, she laid the shirts over her arm, and when she stood up above and the fire was just about to be kindled, she looked about her; there came six swans drawing along through the air.
sentences.i: When she was now led to the stake, she laid the shirts over her arm, and as she stood up above and the fire was just about to be lit, she looked around her — and there came six swans sweeping through the air.
sentences.n: “Scheiterhaufen” = the pyre, the stake for burning. “angezündet werden” = to be kindled (passive). “schaute sich um” is sich umschauen (to look around), separable. “kamen … daher gezogen” = ‘came drawing / sweeping along’ (past participle of manner after kommen).

sentences.t: Da sah sie daß ihre Erlösung nahte und ihr Herz regte sich in Freude.
sentences.l: There she saw that her redemption drew near and her heart stirred in joy.
sentences.i: Then she saw that her deliverance was drawing near, and her heart stirred with joy.
sentences.n: “Erlösung” = redemption, deliverance — the noun of erlösen, the tale’s whole aim. “nahte” is the preterite of nahen (to draw near). “sich regen” = to stir (reflexive).

sentences.t: Die Schwäne rauschten zu ihr her und senkten sich herab so daß sie ihnen die Hemden überwerfen konnte: und wie sie davon berührt wurden, fielen die Schwanenhäute ab, und ihre Brüder standen leibhaftig vor ihr und waren frisch und schön; nur dem jüngsten fehlte der linke Arm, und er hatte dafür einen Schwanenflügel am Rücken.
sentences.l: The swans rushed toward her and lowered themselves down so that she could throw the shirts over them: and as they were touched by them, the swan-skins fell off, and her brothers stood bodily before her and were fresh and fair; only the youngest lacked the left arm, and had instead a swan’s wing on his back.
sentences.i: The swans swept down to her and sank so low that she could cast the shirts over them; and as the shirts touched them, the swan-skins fell away, and her brothers stood before her in the flesh, hale and handsome — only the youngest lacked his left arm, and had a swan’s wing on his back instead.
sentences.n: The planted detail pays off: because the last shirt’s left sleeve was never finished, the youngest brother keeps a swan’s wing where his left arm should be — a permanent mark of how narrowly the redemption was won. “rauschten … her” (herrauschen) and “senkten sich herab” (sich herabsenken) are separable. “überwerfen” = to throw over. “fielen … ab” is abfallen (to fall off). “leibhaftig” = in the flesh, bodily. Note the dative-of-possession “dem jüngsten fehlte der linke Arm” (‘the left arm was lacking to the youngest’).

sentences.t: Sie herzten und küßten sich, und die Königin gieng zu dem Könige, der ganz bestürzt war, und fieng an zu reden und sagte „liebster Gemahl, nun darf ich sprechen und dir offenbaren daß ich unschuldig bin und fälschlich angeklagt,“ und erzählte ihm von dem Betrug der Alten, die ihre drei Kinder weggenommen und verborgen hätte.
sentences.l: They hugged and kissed one another, and the queen went to the king, who was quite dismayed, and began to speak and said, “dearest husband, now I may speak and reveal to you that I am innocent and falsely accused,” and told him of the deceit of the old woman, who had taken away and hidden her three children.
sentences.i: They embraced and kissed one another, and the queen went to the king, who was utterly astonished, and began to speak, and said, “Dearest husband, now I may speak, and reveal to you that I am innocent and falsely accused,” and told him of the old woman’s deceit — how she had carried off and hidden her three children.
sentences.n: The vow breaks at last: the first words she is allowed in six years clear her name. “herzten sich” = hugged one another. “Könige” has the old dative -e. “bestürzt” = dismayed, aghast. “offenbaren” = to reveal. “fälschlich angeklagt” = falsely accused. “weggenommen und verborgen hätte” is subjunctive of reported speech (‘had taken and hidden’).

sentences.t: Da wurden sie zu großer Freude des Königs herbeigeholt, und die böse Schwiegermutter wurde zur Strafe auf den Scheiterhaufen gebunden und zu Asche verbrannt.
sentences.l: There they were fetched, to the great joy of the king, and the wicked mother-in-law was bound to the pyre as punishment and burned to ashes.
sentences.i: Then they were brought in, to the king’s great joy, and the wicked mother-in-law was bound to the stake as punishment and burned to ashes.
sentences.n: The fire prepared for the innocent queen claims the guilty mother-in-law instead — the fairy tale’s exact retribution. “herbeigeholt” is herbeiholen (to fetch over), separable. “zur Strafe” = as punishment. “zu Asche verbrennen” = to burn to ashes.

sentences.t: Der König aber und die Königin mit ihren sechs Brüdern lebten lange Jahre in Glück und Frieden.
sentences.l: But the king and the queen with her six brothers lived long years in happiness and peace.
sentences.i: And the king and the queen, together with her six brothers, lived long years in happiness and peace.
sentences.n: The closing formula, kin to Rapunzel’s ‘glücklich und vergnügt’: “in Glück und Frieden” = in happiness and peace. “Brüdern” is the dative plural of Bruder.
